---
title: "Collocation Analysis Harrisburg and Philly"
author: "Alain Izabayo"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    df-print: kable

editor: visual
---

## 1. Introduction

In this analysis we calculate the r-squared values and root mean sqaure errors of the low-cost sensors used in collocation in Harrisburg and Philadelphia. The goal is to understand how the data collected using the three air sensor types (airgradient, quantaq and clarity) compares to the one collected using EPA monitors and reported to airnow. By tabulating the results we can easily make analyses to understand how the devices perform when compared to EPA monitors. 

Let's first set up some libraries that are likely tobe useful

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
library(tidyverse) #Cleaning data and prdocing basic plots
library(skimr) #For explotary data analysis
library(lubridate) #to help in grouping
library(gt)   #For formatting the table
```

## 2. Data extraction from sensors

The data is extracted from sensors, some cleaning done on it before it is sent to a cloud hosting database known as influxdb. In this notebook we shall read data from influxdb and analyze it from here.

The sensor data from all the three collocation sites was downloaded, cleaned in python and upload to the shared drive. Below we read the csv that stores the data from these sensors.

```{r - bring-the-data}
#| echo: false
#| warning: true
#Read the file into quartz
all_sensor_locations_data = read.csv("~/GitHub/air-quality/harrisburg-philly-aq/data-from-monitors/collocation-report-june26-folder/hbg-philly-collocation-apr-june-2026.csv")
```

We make sure that the data types for each attribute in the dataset is set to the expectation

```{r - explore-the-dataset}
#| echo: false
#Let's look at things like data types to ensure that they are consistent 
str(all_sensor_locations_data, vec.len=2)

```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#Remove the autogenerated index column x
all_sensors_data_clean = subset(all_sensor_locations_data, select = -c(X))

```

R doesnot seem to recognize the time column as time; so we convert it to time datatype for easy use during analysis

```{r - data-type-conversions}
#| echo: false
#Order the data in ascending order by time
all_sensors_data_clean = all_sensors_data_clean[order(all_sensor_locations_data$time_US_eastern), ]

#Convert the time column to date-time format
all_sensors_data_clean$time_US_eastern = as.POSIXct(all_sensors_data_clean$time_US_eastern)

#Check wether the change has been implemented
head(all_sensors_data_clean, n = 3)

```

## 3. Data restructuring for RMSE and R-Squared

Let's filter out for only the devices we plan to use in this analysis. For consistency, we can use the devices that justair is using to calculate the collocation factors.

In addition, we shall also add the EPA monitors since they will be used for reference. Lets first identify the sn for the EPA monitors

```{r - collocation-devices}
#| echo: false
#We first list the selected devices to use whicha are now 
#being used by just air to find correction factors 
correction_devices = c(
  "A93NM49L",
  "MOD-X-00959",
  "MOD-X-00958",
  "A44MFTF3",
  "A636FW6Q",
  "airgradient:588c8135a6ec",
  "airgradient:588c81359ef8",
  "airgradient:588c813fe180"
)

#EPA  monitors should also be included 
#because they will be used for referencing
epa_sn_df = all_sensors_data_clean |>
  distinct(site_name, device_sn, manufacturer) |>
  filter(manufacturer == "EPA") 

#Lets filter so that we have the data 
#for only the correction devices and epa monitors
correction_sensors_hour_df = all_sensors_data_clean |> 
  filter(device_sn %in% c(correction_devices,as.vector(epa_sn_df[,'device_sn'])))

```

Since we need to use 24 hour average data for pm2.5, we can average the data on 24hour

```{r 24hour-df}
#| echo: false
correction_sensors_pm25_24hr = correction_sensors_hour_df |> 
  filter(parameter=="PM2.5") |> 
  mutate(date_US_eastern = as.Date(time_US_eastern)) |> 
  summarise(pm_gases_measures = mean(pm_gases_measures, na.rm=TRUE),
            .by = c(date_US_eastern,site_name, 
                    latitude, longitude, device_sn, 
                     parameter, manufacturer)
            ) 

```

## 4. Calculating the r-squared and rmse

We can first make a table with all the thresholds recommended by epa for comparison

```{r epa-thresholds}
#| echo: false
epa_threshold_table = cbind(Parameter=c("PM2.5","OZONE","NO2","CO"),
                  Avg_period = c("24-hr","1-hr","1-hr","1-hr"),
                  Target.R.squared = c(">=0.70",">=0.80",">=0.70",">=0.80"),
                  Target.rmse = c("<=7µg/m^3","<=5ppb","<=5ppb","<=1ppm"))
epa_threshold_table = data.frame(epa_threshold_table)
```

For each location we find the r-squared and rmse using the EPA data as reference for each low-cost 'representative' device per manufacturer. For reusability and simplicity,let's write a function that directly makes dataframes for r-squared and rmse based on paramter and location

```{r - automation-r-2-rmse}
#| echo: false
find_r2_rmse = function(location_str,
                        param_str, 
                        air_quality_df){
  
  #Ensure that airgradient devices are 
  #not querried for CO as they don't measure it
  if(param_str=="CO"){
    air_quality_df = air_quality_df |> 
      filter(manufacturer != "airgradient") |> 
      #Convert the rest CO values to ppm
      mutate(pm_gases_measures = if_else(parameter=="CO", 
                                         pm_gases_measures/1000,
                                         pm_gases_measures))
  }
  
  #Get the devices at this location for 
  #which the r-squared values are to be determined
  #Execlude the epa devices since they will determined separately
  devices_sn_df = air_quality_df |> 
    filter(site_name==location_str, 
           manufacturer!="EPA") |> 
    subset(select = c("device_sn","manufacturer")) |> 
    distinct(device_sn,manufacturer)
  
  #Make the device sn list vector to allow looping through the devices
  devices_sn_vec = as.vector(devices_sn_df$device_sn)
  #Make the list of manufacturers to use in the table
  manufacturers_vec = as.vector(devices_sn_df$manufacturer)
  
  
  #Filter for data at only this location and for only these parameters
  filtered_for_stats_df = air_quality_df |> 
    filter(site_name==location_str, 
           parameter==param_str) |> 
  #Remove the manufacturer column to prevents duplicates in pivoting 
    select(-c("manufacturer")) |> 
  #Widen the data so as to be able to create x 
  #and y coordinates in fitting the regression models
      pivot_wider(names_from = c(device_sn), 
                  values_from = pm_gases_measures)
  
  
  #Instantiate lists to collect the r-squared, p-values and rmse values
  r_squared_vec = rep(0, length(devices_sn_vec))
  p_values_vec = rep(0, length(devices_sn_vec))
  rmse_vec = rep(0, length(devices_sn_vec))
  
  
  
  #Calculate and collect the r-squared and rmse for each device
  for (i in 1:length(devices_sn_vec)){
    #Obtain the EPA serial number to be used in finding r-squared
    epa_sn_str = unlist(epa_sn_df[epa_sn_df$site_name==
                                    location_str, "device_sn"][1])
    
    #Find the r-squared for each device
    #first ensure that the X and Y variables 
    #in the formular are properly stated 
    formatted_formular = paste0("`",epa_sn_str,"`~`",devices_sn_vec[i],"`")
    
    #Fit the model to find the r^2 and p-value
    model_hbg_phi = lm(as.formula(formatted_formular), 
                        data = filtered_for_stats_df)
    
    #Add the r-squared values to the vector
    r_squared_vec[i] = round(summary(model_hbg_phi)$r.squared,3)
    p_values_vec[i] = format(anova(model_hbg_phi)$"Pr(>F)"[1], 
                             scientific=TRUE
                          )
    
    #Find the root mean square error
    rmse_vec[i] = round(sqrt(mean(resid(model_hbg_phi)^2)),1)
                      
  }
  
  #Properly organize the target values fo incorporation into the table
  Target_df = epa_threshold_table |> 
    filter(Parameter==param_str) |> 
    select(Target.R.squared,Target.rmse)
  #Put all the above in one table including the target values 
  
  combined_df = cbind(
    Location = rep(location_str, length(devices_sn_vec)),
    Device.sn = devices_sn_vec,
    Manufacturer = manufacturers_vec,
    Parameter = rep(param_str, length(devices_sn_vec)),
    R.squared = r_squared_vec,
    P.values = p_values_vec,
    Target.R.squared = rep(
      Target_df$Target.R.squared[1],
      length(devices_sn_vec)
    ),
    
    rmse = rmse_vec,
    Target.rmse = rep(
      Target_df$Target.rmse[1],
      length(devices_sn_vec)
    )
  )
  #Convert it to a dataframe for easy data manipulation
  combined_df = data.frame(combined_df)
  combined_df
}

```

We section the calculations by location for organization.

### 4.1 Harrisburg

The EPA monitors in Harrisburg only measure PM2.5 and Ozone.

#### 4.1.1 Harrisburg PM2.5

We use the function to find the pm2.5 error values in Harrisburg for the three low cost sensors

```{r harrisburg-pm}
#| echo: false
hbg_stats_pm2.5 = find_r2_rmse(
  location_str = "Harrisburg",
  param_str = "PM2.5",
  air_quality_df = correction_sensors_pm25_24hr
)
hbg_stats_pm2.5
```
Based on the values above, we can say that the R.squared and rmse values for all the devices (quantaq, clarity and airgradient) are withing the EPA recommended range

#### 4.1.2 Harrisburg Ozone

```{r harrisburg-ozone}
#| echo: false
hbg_stats_ozone = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Harrisburg", 
                               param_str = "OZONE",
                               air_quality_df = correction_sensors_hour_df )
hbg_stats_ozone

```
The r-squared for Ozone in Harrisburg are low below the EPA recommended threshold. 
Aigradient seems to be having the lowest r-squared value and largest rmse

### 4.2 MON Philadelphia

The Philadelphia Montemogrey site measure PM2.5, CO and NO2.

#### 4.2.1 MON - PM2.5

```{r MON-pm25}
#| echo: false
mon_stats_pm  = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-MON", 
                            param_str = "PM2.5",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_pm25_24hr)
mon_stats_pm
```
Both the air gradient and Clarity at the Montgomery have an r-squared value that is greater than the epa recommended threshold . The rmse is also less than the threshold 

#### 4.2.1 MON - NO2

```{r}
#| echo: false
mon_stats_no2 = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-MON", 
                            param_str = "NO2",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_hour_df)
mon_stats_no2
```
The r-squared values for NO2 at the Montgomery site are way below the thrshold!

#### 4.2.1 MON - CO

```{r}
#| echo: false
mon_stats_co = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-MON", 
                            param_str = "CO",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_hour_df)
mon_stats_co  
```

The CO value for the clarity at Montgomery site is just right on the threshold!

### 4.3 NEW - Philadelphia

#### 4.3.1 NEW - PM2.5

```{r NEW-pm25}
#| echo: false
new_stats_pm  = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-NEW", 
                            param_str = "PM2.5",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_pm25_24hr)
new_stats_pm
```


Surprisingly, the pm2.5 r-squared values on the NEW site are below the threshold. This makes this site the only one where the pm2.5 r-squared values do not meet the threshold. The values are even lower when hourly data is used 

#### 4.3.2 NEW - OZONE

```{r}
#| echo: false
new_stats_ozone = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-NEW", 
                            param_str = "OZONE",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_hour_df)
new_stats_ozone
```

Only airgradient slighlty fails to reach the threshold for the r.squared value for ozone at the NEW site

#### 4.3.3 NEW - NO2

```{r}
#| echo: false
new_stats_no2 = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-NEW", 
                            param_str = "NO2",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_hour_df)
new_stats_no2

```
The r.squared for N02 at the MON site are all below the threshold. SUrprisingly only air gradient is within close to 0.7 and it has the lowest rmse 


#### 4.3.4 NEW - CO

```{r}
#| echo: false
new_stats_co = find_r2_rmse(location_str = "Philly-NEW", 
                            param_str = "CO",
                            air_quality_df = correction_sensors_hour_df)
new_stats_co
```
Both clarity and quantaq CO r-squared values at new are below the threshold with clarity having a slightly higher r-squared

## 5. Summary table of all parameters at all locations 

```{r all-tables-df}
#| echo: false
all_params_hbg_mon_new = bind_rows(
  hbg_stats_pm2.5,
  hbg_stats_ozone,
  mon_stats_pm,
  mon_stats_no2,
  mon_stats_co,
  new_stats_pm,
  new_stats_ozone,
  new_stats_no2,
  new_stats_co
)

all_params_hbg_mon_new

```

```{r - save-the-table}
#| echo: false

write.csv(all_params_hbg_mon_new, 
          file = "r_squared_rmse_hbg_philly.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)
```
